name: Marketing Basics
description: An introduction to core marketing concepts including the marketing mix, consumer behavior, market research, digital marketing, branding, and building a complete marketing plan.

modules.title: What Is Marketing?
modules.objectives: Define marketing and explain its role in business; understand the marketing mix (4Ps); distinguish marketing from selling; describe how marketing creates value for customers and firms.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 1 — Marketing and the Marketing Process
modules.video2Note: YouTube — What Is Marketing? The 4Ps Explained
modules.assignment: Find a real-world advertisement and identify how it uses each of the 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion); write a paragraph explaining the value the product offers to its target customer.

modules.title: Marketing Environment & Research
modules.objectives: Distinguish the micro and macro marketing environments; describe how PESTLE factors affect marketing decisions; explain the steps in the marketing research process and the types of market data.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 2 — Strategic Planning in Marketing
modules.assignment: Choose a business and conduct a PESTLE analysis identifying at least two factors in each category; explain how two of those factors could affect their marketing strategy.

modules.title: Consumer Behavior
modules.objectives: Identify the steps in the consumer buying decision process; explain psychological, social, cultural, and situational factors that influence purchasing decisions; apply consumer behavior insights to marketing strategy.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 3 — Consumer Markets and Buying Behavior
modules.assignment: Walk through the five-step consumer decision process for a recent purchase you made; identify which psychological or social factors most influenced each step and how a marketer could influence your decision.

modules.title: Market Segmentation, Targeting & Positioning
modules.titleZh: 市场细分、目标市场与定位
modules.objectives: Define market segmentation and explain the four major segmentation bases; describe target market selection strategies; construct a positioning statement and perceptual map.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 5 — Market Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning
modules.video2Note: YouTube — STP Marketing: Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Segmentation and Targeting Practice
modules.assignment: Choose a consumer product; segment its market using at least three segmentation bases, select a primary target segment, and write a positioning statement for that segment.

modules.title: Product Strategy
modules.objectives: Distinguish between core, actual, and augmented product levels; explain the product life cycle and its marketing implications; understand product mix decisions including width, length, and depth.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 9 — Products: Consumer and Industrial Goods
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Product Life Cycle: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline
modules.assignment: Select a real product and identify which stage of the product life cycle it is currently in; recommend and justify two specific marketing strategies appropriate for that stage.

modules.title: Pricing Strategy
modules.objectives: Explain internal and external factors affecting pricing decisions; compare pricing strategies including cost-plus, value-based, competitive, penetration, and skimming; understand psychological pricing.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 11 — Pricing Products and Services
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Pricing Strategies: Penetration vs Price Skimming
modules.assignment: A new coffee shop is opening in your city; recommend and justify a pricing strategy for its signature drinks, explaining why you chose it over at least two alternative approaches.

modules.title: Distribution & Place
modules.objectives: Describe marketing channels and their functions; compare direct and indirect distribution; explain channel conflict and management; understand logistics, supply chain, and omnichannel strategies.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 12 — Distribution Channels
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Marketing Channels and Distribution Strategy
modules.assignment: Map the distribution channel for a product of your choice from manufacturer to consumer; identify every intermediary and explain the function each one performs.

modules.title: Promotion & Advertising
modules.objectives: Describe the promotional mix elements (advertising, sales promotion, public relations, personal selling, direct marketing); explain the communication process in marketing; compare push and pull strategies.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 13 — The Promotion Mix
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Advertising vs Public Relations vs Sales Promotion
modules.assignment: Design a simple integrated promotional campaign for a school bake sale; use at least three elements of the promotional mix and explain the role each plays in communicating your message.

modules.title: Digital Marketing & Social Media
modules.objectives: Identify the major digital marketing channels including SEO, email, social media, and content marketing; explain key metrics for digital campaigns; understand influencer marketing and viral content strategies.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 14 — Digital Marketing
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Digital Marketing Strategy for Beginners
modules.assignment: Analyze the social media presence of a brand you follow; evaluate their content strategy, posting frequency, engagement, and use of calls-to-action; recommend two improvements.

modules.title: Branding & Brand Management
modules.objectives: Define brand and explain the components of brand equity; describe strategies for building and maintaining strong brands; analyze brand extension, co-branding, and rebranding decisions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 10 — Branding and Package Design
modules.video2Note: YouTube — What Is Brand Equity and Why Does It Matter?
modules.assignment: Choose a well-known brand and assess its brand equity using the four dimensions (brand loyalty, brand awareness, perceived quality, brand associations); explain what the brand should do to strengthen its weakest dimension.

modules.title: Customer Relationship Management
modules.objectives: Define CRM and its role in creating long-term customer value; explain the customer lifetime value concept; describe loyalty programs and strategies for customer retention and satisfaction.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 1.4 — Relationship Marketing
modules.video2Note: YouTube — CRM: What It Is and Why It Matters
modules.assignment: Design a three-step customer journey map for a local gym showing how a prospect becomes a loyal member; identify the touchpoints and marketing actions at each stage.

modules.title: Marketing Ethics
modules.objectives: Identify ethical issues in marketing including deceptive advertising, privacy violations, and targeting vulnerable populations; understand consumer protection laws; apply an ethical decision-making framework to marketing scenarios.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 16 — Sustainable Marketing
modules.assignment: Find a real example of a marketing campaign that raised ethical concerns; describe the issue, identify which stakeholders were harmed, and explain what the company should have done differently.

modules.title: Building a Marketing Plan
modules.objectives: Describe the components of a marketing plan including situation analysis, objectives, strategy, tactics, and metrics; perform a SWOT analysis; understand how to set SMART marketing objectives.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 2.3 — The Marketing Plan
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Creating a Marketing Plan
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Write a Marketing Plan Step by Step
modules.assignment: Conduct a SWOT analysis for a business idea of your choice; write three SMART marketing objectives and outline the key tactics you would use to achieve each objective.

modules.title: Putting It All Together: Marketing Mix in Action
modules.titleZh: 综合运用：营销组合的实践
modules.objectives: Integrate all 4Ps into a cohesive marketing strategy; analyze a complete marketing case study; evaluate marketing effectiveness and identify areas for improvement.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Marketing Ch 16 — Sustainable Marketing
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Integrated Marketing Strategy Case Study
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Marketing Mix Integration Practice
modules.assignment: Create a complete one-page marketing plan for a student-run business, incorporating all 4Ps, a target segment description, three SMART objectives, and two key performance indicators to measure success.

quizQuestions.question: The four elements of the marketing mix are known as:
quizQuestions.answer: Product, Price, Place, Promotion
quizQuestions.explanation: The classic 4Ps of marketing — Product, Price, Place, and Promotion — form the foundational marketing mix that companies use to market their offerings.

quizQuestions.question: Marketing differs from selling because marketing:
options: Focuses on identifying and satisfying customer needs profitably
Occurs only after a product is made
quizQuestions.answer: Focuses on identifying and satisfying customer needs profitably
quizQuestions.explanation: While selling pushes existing products to customers, marketing takes a broader approach: researching needs, creating products that satisfy those needs, and building long-term relationships.

quizQuestions.question: A value proposition is:
options: The price charged for a product
The promise of value a product delivers to customers
The total revenue from all sales
quizQuestions.answer: The promise of value a product delivers to customers
quizQuestions.explanation: A value proposition explains why customers should choose a company's product over alternatives — the unique combination of benefits it provides.

quizQuestions.question: The 'Place' element of the marketing mix refers to:
options: Where the business headquarters is located
How the product is made available to customers (distribution)
The physical design of the product
quizQuestions.answer: How the product is made available to customers (distribution)
quizQuestions.explanation: Place in the marketing mix concerns how and where products are distributed and made accessible to the target market — including channels, locations, and logistics.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of a service rather than a physical product?
quizQuestions.answer: A haircut
quizQuestions.explanation: Services are intangible — they cannot be seen, touched, or stored. A haircut exemplifies service characteristics: intangibility, inseparability, variability, and perishability.

quizQuestions.question: Primary market research data is:
options: Data already published by other organizations
Original data collected directly for a specific research purpose
quizQuestions.answer: Original data collected directly for a specific research purpose
quizQuestions.explanation: Primary research involves gathering new data firsthand through surveys, interviews, focus groups, or observations — customized to the researcher's specific question.

quizQuestions.question: PESTLE analysis examines which external factors?
options: Product, Elasticity, Supply, Technology, Labor, Expenses
Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental
Price, Employees, Segments, Target, Location, Expansion
Profit, Equity, Sales, Trade, Logistics, Efficiency
quizQuestions.answer: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental
quizQuestions.explanation: PESTLE is a framework for analyzing macro-environmental factors: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, and Environmental.

quizQuestions.question: A focus group in market research involves:
options: A small group discussion moderated to gather qualitative insights
A one-on-one interview with an expert
quizQuestions.answer: A small group discussion moderated to gather qualitative insights
quizQuestions.explanation: Focus groups bring together 6–12 participants to discuss a product, brand, or concept in depth, generating rich qualitative data about attitudes and perceptions.

quizQuestions.question: Secondary market research uses:
options: Data that has already been collected and published by others
quizQuestions.answer: Data that has already been collected and published by others
quizQuestions.explanation: Secondary research analyzes existing data (government statistics, industry reports, academic studies) — faster and cheaper than primary research but less tailored to specific needs.

quizQuestions.question: The main purpose of market research is to:
options: Advertise a product to potential customers
Set the price of a product
quizQuestions.answer: Reduce uncertainty in marketing decision-making
quizQuestions.explanation: Market research gathers information about customers, competitors, and the environment to help managers make better-informed, lower-risk marketing decisions.

quizQuestions.question: The first step in the consumer buying decision process is:
quizQuestions.answer: Problem recognition
quizQuestions.explanation: The buying process begins when the consumer recognizes a need or problem — the gap between their current state and desired state that motivates them to seek a solution.

quizQuestions.question: Social factors that influence consumer behavior include:
options: Family, reference groups, and social class
quizQuestions.answer: Family, reference groups, and social class
quizQuestions.explanation: Social factors include the influence of family, peer groups, social media influencers, and social class on consumer attitudes and purchasing decisions.

quizQuestions.question: Brand loyalty is an example of which consumer behavior factor?
quizQuestions.answer: Psychological factor
quizQuestions.explanation: Brand loyalty relates to learning and attitudes — psychological factors that determine how consumers perceive and respond to brands over time.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive dissonance after a purchase refers to:
options: Doubt or regret a consumer feels after making a purchase
quizQuestions.answer: Doubt or regret a consumer feels after making a purchase
quizQuestions.explanation: Post-purchase cognitive dissonance (buyer's remorse) occurs when consumers question whether they made the right choice, especially for high-involvement purchases.

quizQuestions.question: A consumer is more likely to engage in extensive problem-solving for:
options: A high-involvement purchase like a car or laptop
A product bought out of habit
quizQuestions.answer: A high-involvement purchase like a car or laptop
quizQuestions.explanation: High-involvement purchases are expensive, risky, or personally important — consumers spend more time searching for information and evaluating alternatives.

quizQuestions.question: Demographic segmentation divides markets based on:
quizQuestions.answer: Age, income, gender, and education
quizQuestions.explanation: Demographic segmentation uses measurable population characteristics — age, gender, income, education, occupation, family size — to divide the market.

quizQuestions.question: Psychographic segmentation is based on:
options: Values, interests, lifestyles, and personality traits
quizQuestions.answer: Values, interests, lifestyles, and personality traits
quizQuestions.explanation: Psychographic segmentation groups consumers by psychological characteristics — values, attitudes, interests, and lifestyle choices that influence buying behavior.

quizQuestions.question: A perceptual map is used in marketing to:
options: Show how consumers perceive competing brands on key attributes
quizQuestions.answer: Show how consumers perceive competing brands on key attributes
quizQuestions.explanation: A perceptual map plots brands based on consumer perceptions across two dimensions (e.g., price vs. quality), helping marketers identify competitive positioning gaps.

quizQuestions.question: An undifferentiated marketing strategy targets:
options: Multiple distinct segments with different offers
The entire market with one standard offer
quizQuestions.answer: The entire market with one standard offer
quizQuestions.explanation: An undifferentiated (mass marketing) strategy treats the market as homogeneous and uses one marketing mix for all consumers — efficient but less tailored.

quizQuestions.question: A positioning statement communicates:
options: The unique benefit a product offers to a target segment vs. competitors
The production process of a product
The distribution network of a firm
quizQuestions.answer: The unique benefit a product offers to a target segment vs. competitors
quizQuestions.explanation: A positioning statement defines the target market, the product category, the key benefit, and why consumers should believe it — differentiating the brand in customers' minds.

quizQuestions.question: In the product life cycle, sales grow most rapidly during the:
quizQuestions.answer: Growth stage
quizQuestions.explanation: During the growth stage, consumer awareness increases, sales rise rapidly, and new competitors enter the market attracted by profitability.

quizQuestions.question: The core product refers to:
options: The physical features and packaging of a product
The fundamental benefit or problem-solving service the customer buys
quizQuestions.answer: The fundamental benefit or problem-solving service the customer buys
quizQuestions.explanation: The core product is the essential benefit — what the customer is really buying. A drill's core product is the hole, not the drill itself.

quizQuestions.question: In the maturity stage of the product life cycle, a typical marketing strategy is:
options: Market modification and product differentiation to maintain share
quizQuestions.answer: Market modification and product differentiation to maintain share
quizQuestions.explanation: In maturity, competition is intense and growth slows; marketers focus on retaining market share through product improvements, promotions, and finding new market segments.

quizQuestions.question: Which stage of the product life cycle typically involves the highest marketing costs relative to sales?
quizQuestions.answer: Introduction
quizQuestions.explanation: In the introduction stage, companies must build awareness and distribution, resulting in high marketing costs while sales volume is still low.

quizQuestions.question: Product mix 'depth' refers to:
options: The number of different product lines offered
The number of variants within a single product line
The total number of products sold
quizQuestions.answer: The number of variants within a single product line
quizQuestions.explanation: Product mix depth measures how many variations (sizes, flavors, colors) exist within a single product line — e.g., a shampoo line offered in 8 different formulas.

quizQuestions.question: Cost-plus pricing sets the price by:
options: Adding a markup to the total cost of producing the product
Charging what the customer is willing to pay
Setting price below competitors to gain market share
quizQuestions.answer: Adding a markup to the total cost of producing the product
quizQuestions.explanation: Cost-plus pricing = total cost + desired profit margin. It is simple and ensures costs are covered, but may ignore what customers value or what competitors charge.

quizQuestions.question: Price skimming involves:
options: Setting a low initial price to capture market share quickly
Setting a high initial price and lowering it over time
Setting prices the same as competitors
Offering deep discounts to clear inventory
quizQuestions.answer: Setting a high initial price and lowering it over time
quizQuestions.explanation: Price skimming targets early adopters willing to pay a premium, then lowers price as competition increases and the mass market is targeted.

quizQuestions.question: Penetration pricing is best suited for:
options: Products with a high-income target market
Gaining rapid market share in a price-sensitive market
quizQuestions.answer: Gaining rapid market share in a price-sensitive market
quizQuestions.explanation: Penetration pricing sets a low initial price to attract a large customer base quickly — effective in competitive markets with price-sensitive consumers.

quizQuestions.question: The psychological pricing strategy of charging $9.99 instead of $10 exploits:
options: The tendency for consumers to focus on the leftmost digit of a price
quizQuestions.answer: The tendency for consumers to focus on the leftmost digit of a price
quizQuestions.explanation: Charm pricing (odd-even pricing) works because consumers perceive $9.99 as significantly less than $10 — anchoring on the leftmost digit.

quizQuestions.question: Value-based pricing sets price based on:
options: The perceived value of the product to the customer
quizQuestions.answer: The perceived value of the product to the customer
quizQuestions.explanation: Value-based pricing aligns price with the economic value customers place on a product — often allowing premium pricing for high-differentiation products.

quizQuestions.question: An intermediary in a distribution channel:
options: Connects producers to consumers by performing distribution functions
quizQuestions.answer: Connects producers to consumers by performing distribution functions
quizQuestions.explanation: Intermediaries (wholesalers, retailers, agents) add value by performing functions like storage, transportation, promotion, and breaking bulk between producer and consumer.

quizQuestions.question: Direct distribution means the producer:
options: Sells directly to the end consumer without intermediaries
quizQuestions.answer: Sells directly to the end consumer without intermediaries
quizQuestions.explanation: Direct distribution (e.g., a company website or factory store) eliminates intermediaries, giving the producer greater control over customer experience and higher margins.

quizQuestions.question: Intensive distribution means placing products in:
options: A select number of carefully chosen retailers
quizQuestions.answer: As many outlets as possible
quizQuestions.explanation: Intensive distribution maximizes product availability by placing it in every possible outlet — common for convenience goods like snacks, gum, or beverages.

quizQuestions.question: An omnichannel strategy integrates:
options: Multiple sales channels so customers have a seamless experience
quizQuestions.answer: Multiple sales channels so customers have a seamless experience
quizQuestions.explanation: Omnichannel retailing seamlessly connects online stores, mobile apps, and physical stores so customers can interact with a brand consistently across all channels.

quizQuestions.question: Channel conflict occurs when:
options: Two or more channel members disagree about roles, pricing, or territory
A company expands to new markets
quizQuestions.answer: Two or more channel members disagree about roles, pricing, or territory
quizQuestions.explanation: Channel conflict — between manufacturer and retailer, or between two retailers — can disrupt distribution and must be managed through clear agreements or channel policies.

quizQuestions.question: Which element of the promotional mix involves mass communication through paid media?
quizQuestions.answer: Advertising
quizQuestions.explanation: Advertising is paid, non-personal mass communication through media such as TV, radio, print, digital, and outdoor — used to reach large audiences.

quizQuestions.question: A push promotional strategy focuses on:
options: Creating consumer demand that pulls products through channels
Persuading intermediaries to stock and promote the product
Using social media to engage customers directly
Building brand loyalty among end users
quizQuestions.answer: Persuading intermediaries to stock and promote the product
quizQuestions.explanation: A push strategy 'pushes' products through the distribution channel using trade promotions, personal selling, and incentives directed at retailers and wholesalers.

quizQuestions.question: Sales promotion includes tactics such as:
options: Coupons, rebates, contests, and buy-one-get-one offers
quizQuestions.answer: Coupons, rebates, contests, and buy-one-get-one offers
quizQuestions.explanation: Sales promotions are short-term incentives designed to stimulate immediate purchasing — including coupons, sweepstakes, samples, and temporary price reductions.

quizQuestions.question: Public relations (PR) differs from advertising primarily because PR:
options: Uses earned media rather than paid media
quizQuestions.answer: Uses earned media rather than paid media
quizQuestions.explanation: PR generates unpaid media coverage (news stories, reviews, endorsements), which often carries more credibility than paid advertising.

quizQuestions.question: Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC) ensures that:
options: A company uses only one promotional medium
All promotional messages are consistent and reinforce each other across channels
Marketing and finance departments share the same budget
Only digital marketing channels are used
quizQuestions.answer: All promotional messages are consistent and reinforce each other across channels
quizQuestions.explanation: IMC coordinates all marketing communication tools — advertising, PR, social media, packaging, personal selling — to deliver a unified, consistent message.

quizQuestions.question: SEO stands for:
quizQuestions.answer: Search Engine Optimization
quizQuestions.explanation: SEO is the process of improving a website's visibility in organic (non-paid) search engine results to drive more traffic.

quizQuestions.question: Click-through rate (CTR) in digital marketing measures:
options: The total number of website visitors per day
The percentage of people who click an ad after seeing it
The cost of each ad click
The number of social media followers
quizQuestions.answer: The percentage of people who click an ad after seeing it
quizQuestions.explanation: CTR = (clicks / impressions) × 100. It measures how compelling an ad or link is — a higher CTR indicates more relevant, engaging content.

quizQuestions.question: Influencer marketing works primarily by:
options: Leveraging the credibility and audience of social media personalities to promote products
quizQuestions.answer: Leveraging the credibility and audience of social media personalities to promote products
quizQuestions.explanation: Influencer marketing taps into an influencer's trust relationship with their followers to promote products authentically — often more effective than traditional ads for younger audiences.

quizQuestions.question: Content marketing involves:
options: Paying for ad space in traditional media
Creating and distributing valuable, relevant content to attract and retain a target audience
Buying followers on social media platforms
quizQuestions.answer: Creating and distributing valuable, relevant content to attract and retain a target audience
quizQuestions.explanation: Content marketing provides value to potential customers through blogs, videos, podcasts, and infographics — building trust and brand authority rather than directly selling.

quizQuestions.question: A conversion in digital marketing refers to:
options: Converting a social post into a paid ad
A visitor completing a desired action (purchase, sign-up, download)
quizQuestions.answer: A visitor completing a desired action (purchase, sign-up, download)
quizQuestions.explanation: A conversion occurs when a user takes the desired action — buying, subscribing, or downloading — indicating the marketing effort achieved its goal.

quizQuestions.question: Brand equity refers to:
options: The monetary value of a company's physical assets
The added value a brand name gives a product beyond its functional benefits
The total advertising budget of a company
The number of products in a brand's portfolio
quizQuestions.answer: The added value a brand name gives a product beyond its functional benefits
quizQuestions.explanation: Brand equity is the premium consumers are willing to pay for a branded product over a generic equivalent — built through awareness, loyalty, perceived quality, and associations.

quizQuestions.question: Co-branding is a strategy where:
options: A company creates a new brand from scratch
Two or more brands collaborate on a single product
A brand is extended to a new product category
A brand reduces its product line
quizQuestions.answer: Two or more brands collaborate on a single product
quizQuestions.explanation: Co-branding (e.g., Nike + Apple for Apple Watch Nike) combines two brands' strengths to create a product that benefits from both brands' equity.

quizQuestions.question: Brand awareness is important because:
options: It is the first step in building customer preference and purchase intent
It eliminates the need for product quality
It is only relevant for new products
quizQuestions.answer: It is the first step in building customer preference and purchase intent
quizQuestions.explanation: Consumers must first know a brand exists before they can develop positive attitudes or buy it — brand awareness is the foundation of brand equity.

quizQuestions.question: Brand extension occurs when:
options: A company creates an entirely new brand
An existing brand name is used for a new product in a different category
A brand reduces its geographic reach
quizQuestions.answer: An existing brand name is used for a new product in a different category
quizQuestions.explanation: Brand extension leverages an established brand's recognition and trust to enter a new market — e.g., Apple entering headphones with AirPods.

quizQuestions.question: A strong brand name should be:
options: Long and descriptive of all product features
Identical to a competitor's brand name
Memorable, easy to pronounce, and distinctive
quizQuestions.answer: Memorable, easy to pronounce, and distinctive
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective brand names are short, memorable, distinctive, and easy to pronounce — making it easy for consumers to recognize and recall the brand.

quizQuestions.question: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) represents:
options: The profit from a single transaction
The total net profit a company expects from a customer over the entire relationship
The cost of acquiring a new customer
quizQuestions.answer: The total net profit a company expects from a customer over the entire relationship
quizQuestions.explanation: CLV measures the long-term value of retaining customers — it shows why investing in customer relationships is often more profitable than constantly acquiring new ones.

quizQuestions.question: CRM software primarily helps businesses:
options: Track and manage customer interactions and relationships
quizQuestions.answer: Track and manage customer interactions and relationships
quizQuestions.explanation: CRM (Customer Relationship Management) systems help businesses organize customer data, track purchase history, personalize communications, and improve service quality.

quizQuestions.question: Customer retention is generally more profitable than customer acquisition because:
options: Existing customers always spend more money
Acquiring new customers costs 5–7 times more than retaining existing ones
Retained customers are more likely to complain
Acquisition is illegal in most markets
quizQuestions.answer: Acquiring new customers costs 5–7 times more than retaining existing ones
quizQuestions.explanation: Research consistently shows that retaining existing customers is far less expensive than attracting new ones — and loyal customers often spend more and refer others.

quizQuestions.question: A loyalty program is designed to:
options: Reduce marketing costs by eliminating promotions
Reward repeat customers and encourage ongoing purchasing
quizQuestions.answer: Reward repeat customers and encourage ongoing purchasing
quizQuestions.explanation: Loyalty programs (points, tiers, exclusive rewards) incentivize repeat purchases and deepen the emotional and transactional relationship with the brand.

quizQuestions.question: Net Promoter Score (NPS) measures:
options: How likely customers are to recommend the company to others
quizQuestions.answer: How likely customers are to recommend the company to others
quizQuestions.explanation: NPS asks customers 'How likely are you to recommend us?' on a 0–10 scale; Promoters (9–10) minus Detractors (0–6) gives the NPS — a key indicator of customer loyalty.

quizQuestions.question: Deceptive advertising involves:
options: Making false or misleading claims about a product
quizQuestions.answer: Making false or misleading claims about a product
quizQuestions.explanation: Deceptive advertising misleads consumers about a product's nature, qualities, benefits, or price — violating FTC regulations and consumer trust.

quizQuestions.question: Marketing to children raises ethical concerns primarily because:
options: Children may lack the cognitive ability to critically evaluate marketing messages
quizQuestions.answer: Children may lack the cognitive ability to critically evaluate marketing messages
quizQuestions.explanation: Young children often cannot distinguish advertising from entertainment or critically evaluate persuasion attempts, making them a vulnerable population that requires ethical consideration.

quizQuestions.question: Greenwashing refers to:
options: Falsely claiming products are eco-friendly for marketing purposes
quizQuestions.answer: Falsely claiming products are eco-friendly for marketing purposes
quizQuestions.explanation: Greenwashing misleads consumers into thinking a product or company is more environmentally responsible than it actually is, exploiting growing environmental consciousness.

quizQuestions.question: The FTC (Federal Trade Commission) regulates advertising in the US to:
options: Set prices for all consumer goods
Ensure advertising is truthful and not deceptive
quizQuestions.answer: Ensure advertising is truthful and not deceptive
quizQuestions.explanation: The FTC enforces consumer protection laws, requiring advertising to be truthful, substantiated, and not misleading — protecting consumers from fraudulent marketing practices.

quizQuestions.question: Cause-related marketing involves:
options: Marketing products that cause health problems
A company linking its sales to a charitable cause
Research into the causes of market failure
quizQuestions.answer: A company linking its sales to a charitable cause
quizQuestions.explanation: Cause-related marketing ties product purchases to charitable donations, generating positive PR and appealing to consumers who want their purchases to have social impact.

quizQuestions.question: A SWOT analysis examines a company's:
quizQuestions.answer: Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats
quizQuestions.explanation: SWOT analysis evaluates internal factors (Strengths, Weaknesses) and external factors (Opportunities, Threats) to inform strategic marketing planning.

quizQuestions.question: A SMART marketing objective must be:
quizQuestions.answer: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
quizQuestions.explanation: SMART objectives give clear direction for marketing efforts: they specify exactly what is to be achieved (Specific), how success will be measured (Measurable), within a realistic timeframe (Time-bound).

quizQuestions.question: The situation analysis in a marketing plan typically includes:
options: A SWOT analysis and review of the marketing environment
quizQuestions.answer: A SWOT analysis and review of the marketing environment
quizQuestions.explanation: The situation analysis is the foundation of a marketing plan — it assesses where the company stands now using SWOT, competitive analysis, and environmental scanning.

quizQuestions.question: Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in marketing are used to:
options: Measure whether marketing objectives are being achieved
quizQuestions.answer: Measure whether marketing objectives are being achieved
quizQuestions.explanation: KPIs are quantifiable metrics (sales growth, market share, conversion rate, CLV) that allow marketers to track progress toward their objectives.

quizQuestions.question: Which section of the marketing plan outlines HOW the company will achieve its objectives?
quizQuestions.answer: Marketing strategy and tactics
quizQuestions.explanation: The strategy section describes the overall approach (target market, positioning), while tactics specify the specific actions — campaigns, promotions, pricing — that will implement the strategy.

quizQuestions.question: An integrated marketing strategy ensures that:
options: Different departments have separate marketing budgets
All 4Ps are aligned with each other and with the target market's needs
quizQuestions.answer: All 4Ps are aligned with each other and with the target market's needs
quizQuestions.explanation: An effective marketing strategy integrates Product, Price, Place, and Promotion so they work together coherently to deliver the right value to the target customer.

quizQuestions.question: Return on Marketing Investment (ROMI) is calculated as:
options: Marketing costs divided by number of ads
Net profit from marketing minus marketing costs, divided by marketing costs
Total sales divided by marketing budget
Customer satisfaction score divided by ad spend
quizQuestions.answer: Net profit from marketing minus marketing costs, divided by marketing costs
quizQuestions.explanation: ROMI = (Net profit attributable to marketing − Marketing investment) / Marketing investment × 100. It measures the financial return generated by marketing activities.

quizQuestions.question: A/B testing in marketing involves:
options: Comparing two versions of a marketing element to see which performs better
Testing a product in two different markets simultaneously
Asking A-list celebrities and B-list celebrities to endorse a product
quizQuestions.answer: Comparing two versions of a marketing element to see which performs better
quizQuestions.explanation: A/B testing (split testing) shows version A to one group and version B to another, measuring which drives better results — allowing data-driven marketing optimization.

quizQuestions.question: Market share is calculated as:
options: A firm's total revenue divided by its costs
A firm's sales as a percentage of total industry sales
The number of customers divided by the population
Annual growth rate of a company
quizQuestions.answer: A firm's sales as a percentage of total industry sales
quizQuestions.explanation: Market share = (Company sales / Total market sales) × 100. It measures a company's relative competitive position within its industry.

quizQuestions.question: A company that continually researches customer needs and adjusts its marketing mix is practicing:
quizQuestions.answer: Marketing orientation
quizQuestions.explanation: A marketing-oriented company puts customer needs at the center of all decisions, continuously gathering market intelligence and adapting its offerings to satisfy customers better than competitors.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the 4Ps of the marketing mix?
questions.answer: Profit
questions.explanation: The 4Ps are Product, Price, Place, and Promotion. Profit is an outcome of marketing success, not an element of the marketing mix.

questions.question: Which type of market research involves direct observation of consumers in their natural setting?
questions.answer: Ethnographic research
questions.explanation: Ethnographic research involves researchers observing consumers in their natural environment to understand behavior as it actually occurs — providing deep contextual insights.

questions.question: Post-purchase behavior is important to marketers because:
options: It determines the initial price of a product
Satisfied customers create word-of-mouth referrals and repeat purchases
It occurs before the consumer makes a purchase decision
It is only relevant for luxury goods
questions.answer: Satisfied customers create word-of-mouth referrals and repeat purchases
questions.explanation: Post-purchase satisfaction drives customer loyalty and word-of-mouth, while dissatisfaction leads to returns and negative reviews — both critical to brand reputation.

questions.question: Behavioral segmentation divides markets based on:
options: Purchase behavior, usage rate, and brand loyalty
questions.answer: Purchase behavior, usage rate, and brand loyalty
questions.explanation: Behavioral segmentation groups consumers by actual purchase behavior — how often they buy, how much they use, and how loyal they are to specific brands.

questions.question: In the introduction stage of the product life cycle, profits are typically:
options: Negative or very low due to high launch costs
questions.answer: Negative or very low due to high launch costs
questions.explanation: During introduction, sales are low and marketing/distribution costs are high, typically resulting in losses or minimal profit.

questions.question: A company prices a new product at $199 to signal quality while remaining just below $200. This is an example of:
questions.answer: Psychological pricing
questions.explanation: Pricing at $199 uses the just-below effect — consumers perceive it as significantly less than $200, while the .99 ending can also signal a deal.

questions.question: Exclusive distribution (like a Rolex boutique) is most appropriate for:
questions.answer: Luxury or specialty goods
questions.explanation: Exclusive distribution limits a product to a very small number of prestigious outlets, reinforcing the brand's luxury image and exclusivity.

questions.question: Which promotional element is most effective for complex, high-value B2B sales?
questions.answer: Personal selling
questions.explanation: Complex B2B sales require relationship-building, needs assessment, and tailored solutions — strengths of personal selling that mass advertising cannot replicate.

questions.question: Which metric measures the percentage of website visitors who take a desired action?
questions.answer: Conversion rate
questions.explanation: Conversion rate = (conversions / total visitors) × 100. It measures the effectiveness of a website or campaign in achieving its goal (purchase, sign-up, etc.).

questions.question: The primary purpose of sales promotions is to:
options: Generate immediate consumer action or purchase
Replace advertising in the promotional mix
questions.answer: Generate immediate consumer action or purchase
questions.explanation: Sales promotions use short-term incentives (discounts, coupons, contests) to stimulate quick buying decisions — complementing the longer-term brand-building of advertising.

questions.question: A SWOT analysis identifies Opportunities as:
options: External factors the company can exploit to its advantage
questions.answer: External factors the company can exploit to its advantage
questions.explanation: Opportunities in SWOT are external — trends, market gaps, regulatory changes, or competitor weaknesses that the company can capitalize on.

questions.question: Brand loyalty is valuable to a company because loyal customers:
options: Are always the most profitable segment
Are less price-sensitive and generate repeat revenue
questions.answer: Are less price-sensitive and generate repeat revenue
questions.explanation: Loyal customers tend to repurchase consistently, pay premium prices, require less persuasion, and often recommend the brand to others.

questions.question: The 'augmented product' level includes:
options: The core benefit the consumer buys
Additional features like warranty, delivery, and after-sale service
questions.answer: Additional features like warranty, delivery, and after-sale service
questions.explanation: The augmented product goes beyond the physical product to include extra services and benefits — delivery, installation, warranty, and customer support — that differentiate offerings.

questions.question: Geographic segmentation divides markets by:
options: Location such as country, region, city, or climate
questions.answer: Location such as country, region, city, or climate
questions.explanation: Geographic segmentation recognizes that customer needs, preferences, and behaviors often vary by location — from country-level cultural differences to local climate needs.

questions.question: A pull promotional strategy aims to:
options: Push products through wholesalers to retailers
Create consumer demand that pulls products through the distribution channel
Reduce the number of distribution intermediaries
questions.answer: Create consumer demand that pulls products through the distribution channel
questions.explanation: A pull strategy targets end consumers directly with advertising and promotions, creating demand that causes retailers to stock the product in response to consumer requests.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes relationship marketing?
options: Focusing exclusively on making single transactions
Building long-term mutually beneficial relationships with customers
Reducing the price to attract new buyers
questions.answer: Building long-term mutually beneficial relationships with customers
questions.explanation: Relationship marketing prioritizes long-term customer loyalty over individual transactions, recognizing that retention and lifetime value create more sustainable profitability.

questions.question: Which digital marketing channel involves earning placement in search results without paying per click?
questions.answer: Search Engine Optimization (SEO)
questions.explanation: SEO improves a website's organic (free) rankings in search results through relevant content, site structure, and backlinks — distinct from paid search advertising.

questions.question: Which segmentation strategy is likely used by Netflix to recommend content?
options: Behavioral and psychographic segmentation using viewing data
questions.answer: Behavioral and psychographic segmentation using viewing data
questions.explanation: Netflix uses algorithms analyzing viewing history, ratings, and preferences (behavioral and psychographic data) to personalize recommendations for each subscriber.

questions.question: When a cereal brand introduces a new granola bar product, this is an example of:
questions.answer: Brand extension
questions.explanation: Brand extension uses an existing brand name in a new product category — leveraging established brand recognition while diversifying the product portfolio.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered unethical targeting in marketing?
options: Targeting college students with textbook discounts
Targeting elderly consumers with misleading high-pressure timeshare sales tactics
Targeting families with children's educational products
Targeting fitness enthusiasts with gym memberships
questions.answer: Targeting elderly consumers with misleading high-pressure timeshare sales tactics
questions.explanation: Targeting vulnerable populations (elderly, children, financially distressed) with deceptive or manipulative tactics violates ethical marketing principles and often legal standards.

questions.question: The marketing concept holds that firms should:
options: Produce as much as possible to lower unit costs
Focus on making sales regardless of customer needs
Identify and satisfy customer needs more effectively than competitors
Maximize short-term profit above all else
questions.answer: Identify and satisfy customer needs more effectively than competitors
questions.explanation: The marketing concept states that organizational success comes from determining the needs of target markets and delivering desired satisfactions better than competitors.

questions.question: A company with a high NPS (Net Promoter Score) likely has:
options: A large number of loyal customers who recommend the brand to others
questions.answer: A large number of loyal customers who recommend the brand to others
questions.explanation: A high NPS indicates more promoters (9–10 ratings) than detractors (0–6) — customers who actively recommend the brand, driving organic growth.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a push promotional strategy?
options: A consumer campaign asking fans to share a hashtag
A TV commercial targeting end consumers
A trade promotion offering retailers discounts for prominently displaying the product
questions.answer: A trade promotion offering retailers discounts for prominently displaying the product
questions.explanation: Push strategies direct promotions at channel intermediaries (retailers, wholesalers) to encourage them to stock and promote the product — 'pushing' it toward consumers.

questions.question: When a store like Walmart sells products under its own brand name (e.g., Great Value), this is called:
questions.answer: Private label branding
questions.explanation: Private label (store brand) products are manufactured by a third party but sold under the retailer's own brand name — typically at a lower price than national brands.

questions.question: Content marketing primarily generates value by:
options: Providing useful, relevant information that attracts and engages the target audience
questions.answer: Providing useful, relevant information that attracts and engages the target audience
questions.explanation: Content marketing builds trust and brand authority by offering genuinely useful content — rather than selling directly — establishing the brand as a knowledgeable resource.

questions.question: A marketing channel that goes from manufacturer → wholesaler → retailer → consumer is called:
questions.answer: A two-level channel
questions.explanation: Channel levels count the number of intermediaries. Manufacturer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer has two intermediaries (wholesaler and retailer), making it a two-level channel.

questions.question: The decline stage of the product life cycle is characterized by:
options: Rapidly increasing sales and new competitors
Falling sales, shrinking market, and possible product discontinuation
questions.answer: Falling sales, shrinking market, and possible product discontinuation
questions.explanation: In the decline stage, sales fall due to changing consumer preferences, technology shifts, or new alternatives — firms must decide whether to harvest, divest, or rejuvenate the product.

questions.question: Cost-plus pricing guarantees coverage of costs but may fail because it:
options: Ignores what customers value and what competitors charge
questions.answer: Ignores what customers value and what competitors charge
questions.explanation: Cost-plus pricing is internally focused; it may result in prices that are too high (losing sales) or too low (leaving money on the table) if market realities are not considered.

questions.question: Greenwashing is problematic because it:
options: Makes products more expensive to manufacture
Misleads consumers who want to make environmentally responsible choices
questions.answer: Misleads consumers who want to make environmentally responsible choices
questions.explanation: Greenwashing exploits consumers' environmental values with false or exaggerated eco-friendly claims, eroding trust and undermining genuine sustainability efforts.

questions.question: Which of the following is the BEST example of a SMART marketing objective?
options: Grow Instagram followers by 20% within 3 months through a daily posting schedule
questions.answer: Grow Instagram followers by 20% within 3 months through a daily posting schedule
questions.explanation: This objective is Specific (Instagram followers), Measurable (20%), Achievable (daily posts), Relevant (social media growth), and Time-bound (3 months).

questions.question: Market penetration as a growth strategy involves:
options: Developing new products for existing markets
Selling more of existing products to existing customers
questions.answer: Selling more of existing products to existing customers
questions.explanation: Market penetration (from Ansoff's Matrix) aims to grow by increasing market share — selling more of what you already have to customers you already serve.

questions.question: Which consumer buying decision involves the LEAST deliberation?
options: Grabbing a candy bar at checkout
questions.answer: Grabbing a candy bar at checkout
questions.explanation: Low-involvement routine purchases (like snacks) involve habitual buying with little deliberation — opposite to high-involvement decisions like homes or cars.

questions.question: The purpose of competitive analysis in a marketing plan is to:
options: Understand rival firms' strengths, weaknesses, and strategies to find competitive advantage
questions.answer: Understand rival firms' strengths, weaknesses, and strategies to find competitive advantage
questions.explanation: Competitive analysis informs positioning and strategy by revealing where rivals are strong or weak, what they offer, and where market opportunities exist.

questions.question: Social proof in marketing (testimonials, user reviews) works by:
options: Leveraging other consumers' positive experiences to reduce risk and build trust
questions.answer: Leveraging other consumers' positive experiences to reduce risk and build trust
questions.explanation: Social proof reduces purchase risk by showing prospects that others like them have bought and been satisfied — one of the most powerful forms of persuasion in marketing.

questions.question: A brand's positioning is most effective when it is:
options: Relevant to the target segment, distinctive from competitors, and credibly delivered
questions.answer: Relevant to the target segment, distinctive from competitors, and credibly delivered
questions.explanation: Effective positioning occupies a unique, valued, and believable place in the minds of target customers — differentiating the brand in a way that drives preference and loyalty.
